He said: "We had horses that reproduced, and gifts that accumulated," Abu Huraira says. He said: "I have calculated your provision and your sustenance, and this is an excess, so pay it back." He said: "It does not belong to you." He said: "Yes it does, and by Allah I will cause pain to your back!" **Then he stood up to him with the whip and struck him until he made him bleed!** Then he said: "Bring it." He said: "I seek its reward with Allah." He said: "That would be if you took it from lawful means and paid it back willingly. Did you come from the farthest stone of Bahrain so the people collect taxes for you? No, and not for the Muslims! Umayma did not bring you back except for grazing donkeys!" Umayma is the mother of Abu Huraira; grazing donkeys meaning he herds donkeys. "Umayma did not bring you back except for grazing donkeys!" The point of evidence here: "Then he stood up to him with the whip and struck him until he made him bleed!" He made Abu Huraira bleed. And this issue is a known issue, and its sources are known. And Sheikh al-Amini spoke for several pages about models of this issue, and I do not have time to deal with these details; the viewer can review them from page 382. This topic continues, and from various sources, to page 390.

Two men passed by him... and this is also in Kanz al-Ummal. All these reports are present in Kanz al-Ummal and in other than Kanz al-Ummal. Two men passed by Caliph Umar while he was inspecting the camels of charity. So he said to them: "Where did you come from?" They said: "From Bayt al-Maqdis (Jerusalem)." **So he struck them both with the whip!** Struck them both, meaning he beat them on their heads, on their backs. "So he struck them both with the whip, and said: 'Is there a pilgrimage like the pilgrimage to the House (Kaaba)?'" He assumed this assumption without knowing whether they went with this intention or not! They said: "We were only passing through! We passed by Bayt al-Maqdis as a transit, we crossed through it." You notice the beating in every direction and in every situation!

The issue of Sabigh al-Iraqi, and it is mentioned in numerous sources. This is the one who used to ask about details regarding the meanings of the verses of the Noble Book, and Umar used to reject that. So Umar sent for wet palm branches and struck him with them. Wet palm branches, meaning fresh branches that had not dried yet. "So he sent for wet palm branches and struck him with them until he left his back ulcerated." Ulcerated, meaning his entire back broke out in sores, until it ulcerated and festered. "So Umar sent for wet palm branches and struck him with them until he left his back ulcerated." This is in Sunan al-Darimi and in others too. **"Then he left him until he healed. Then he returned to him! Then he left him until he healed."** Meaning he beats him until he festers, then a second time—it seems he was imprisoned, he detained him—then he returns to him and beats him! "He struck him with them until he left his back ulcerated, then he left him until he healed, then he returned to him, then he left him until he healed. Then he called for him to return to him! So Sabigh said: 'If you want to kill me, then kill me a beautiful killing. And if you want to cure me, then by Allah I have been cured.' So he permitted him to go to his land, and wrote to Abu Musa al-Ash'ari..." to the end of the issue. Just questions! He was just raising questions regarding some meanings of the Quranic verses. And the detail is present, and the sources which Sheikh al-Amini pointed to with volume and page numbers are very many.

Sheikh al-Amini also transmits: And al-Dhahabi extracted in Al-Tadhkirah from Abu Salama, he said: "I said to Abu Huraira: 'Did you use to narrate in the time of Umar like this?'" Abu Salama asks Abu Huraira after the caliphate of Umar, he asks him: "Did you use to narrate in the time of Umar like this?" Considering that Umar forbade the hadith, forbade the spreading of the hadith of the Messenger of Allah. "So he said: **'If I were to narrate in the time of Umar like I narrate to you, he would have struck me with his whip!'**" And Abu Umar extracted from Abu Huraira: **"I have narrated to you hadiths, if I had narrated them in the time of Umar ibn al-Khattab, Umar would have struck me with the whip."** And in the wording of al-Zuhri: "Would I be narrating these hadiths to you while Umar is alive? Nay, by Allah, then I would be certain that the whip would touch my back." And in the wording of Ibn Wahb: **"I narrate hadiths, if I spoke them in the time of Umar or near Umar, he would have cracked my head open!"** All of this is in Tarikh Ibn Kathir. "And Abu Huraira also said"—in Tarikh Ibn Kathir—"And Abu Huraira said: **'We were not able to say 'The Messenger of Allah said' until Umar died.'**" All of this is in Tarikh Ibn Kathir, the eighth volume, page 115, in the history of the events of the year 59 Hijri. Abu Huraira says: "We were not able to say 'The Messenger of Allah said' until Umar died." Because he forbade that the hadith of the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, be published! And here are a thousand question marks!

From Abu Amr al-Shaybani, he said: Umar ibn al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him, was informed of a man who fasts continuously. Meaning continuous fasting. **"So he began striking him with his whip, saying: 'Eat, O continuous faster! Eat, O continuous faster!'"** And the sources are numerous and many, pointed to by Sheikh al-Amini, may Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon him. These are quick models of what Sheikh al-Amini brought in his book Al-Ghadir, I snatched them from here and there.
Segment 4: The Harshness of the Caliph in the Eyes of the Companions and the People

Sheikh al-Ghizzi shifts to examining the broader public perception of Umar's harshness. He cites Tarikh al-Tabari, recounting Umar's shocking dying command regarding the Shura council: to behead any of the prominent Companions who disagreed with the majority. The Sheikh then highlights the testimonies of women—specifically Umm Kulthum bint Abi Bakr and Umm Aban—who flatly rejected Umar's marriage proposals, describing him as a man who "enters frowning and exits frowning," and who is "severe upon women." Finally, he quotes Kanz al-Ummal, showing that the Companions themselves protested Abu Bakr's appointment of Umar due to his extreme roughness, and even an ordinary Bedouin merchant explicitly called the Caliph a "man of evil" after witnessing his abusive behavior toward animals.

Let us go to what came in the books of the people as an example. This is Tarikh al-Tabari, and this is the second volume according to the Dar Sader edition. I will quickly point to his will to Suhayb in the issue of the Shura (the electoral council), and it passed upon us. He said to him: "And stand over their heads. If five gather and agree on a man, and one refuses, then crush his head, or strike his head with the sword. And if four agree and consent to a man among them, and two refuse, then strike their heads. And if three agree on a man among them, and three on a man among them, then make Abdullah ibn Umar the judge. So whichever of the two groups he judges for, let them choose a man from them. If they do not agree to the judgment of Abdullah ibn Umar, then be with those among whom is Abdul Rahman ibn Awf, **and kill the rest!**"

A process of smashing heads, killing, striking with the sword! And are these not the "Ten Promised Paradise"? Are these not the "Ten Promised Paradise" who were in the Shura council which Umar ibn al-Khattab convened?

When we go to page 739 of the second volume—and some of these events passed upon us in the Fatimi File. "And he proposed to Umm Kulthum bint Abi Bakr, and she was young. And he sent regarding her to Aisha. So she said: 'The matter is up to you' (addressing Umm Kulthum). So Umm Kulthum said: 'I have no need of him.' So Aisha said to her: 'Do you turn away from the Commander of the Believers?' She said: **'Yes, indeed he is harsh in living, severe upon women,'**" to the end of the speech. Then Amr ibn al-Aas intervenes. The point of evidence here is this picture that was known about Caliph Umar, that he is harsh in living, severe upon women.

When Amr ibn al-Aas intervened in the issue, what did he say to Umar ibn al-Khattab? He said to him: "But she is a young girl, raised under the care of the Mother of the Believers in softness and gentleness. And in you is harshness, and we are in awe of you, and we cannot turn you away from any of your character traits." Meaning this issue is a known issue, the issue of bad character is a clear issue in the life of Caliph Umar. Therefore, when he proposed to Umm Aban bint Utbah ibn Rabi'ah, also mentioned on page 799, she hated him. She hated the Caliph and said: **"He locks his door, prevents his good, enters frowning, and exits frowning."** These are the descriptions of the Caliph in the eyes of the people who lived in his time, and in the eyes of women specifically, as in these texts. She said: "He is harsh in living, severe upon women." This is Umm Kulthum bint Abi Bakr, and the family of Abu Bakr is the closest to the family of Umar, they know his details; some people know the details of what is in the houses of others. And Umm Aban bint Utbah said, after she hated his proposal, she hated it and said: "He locks his door, prevents his good, enters frowning, and exits frowning."

And the matter does not stop at this limit. When we read, for example, in Kanz al-Ummal, this is the fifth volume of Kanz al-Ummal, Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah edition, page 269: "And some of the companions of the Prophet heard of the entering of Abdul Rahman and Uthman..." Abdul Rahman, meaning Abdul Rahman ibn Awf, "...upon Abu Bakr and their being alone with him. So they entered upon Abu Bakr, and a speaker from among them—from the Companions—said to him: **'What will you say to your Lord when He asks you about appointing Umar over us as a successor, while you have seen his harshness?'** So Abu Bakr said: 'Sit me up...'" to the end of the speech. Meaning this issue was clear: the harshness and the roughness. They object to his succession to the Messenger of Allah.

On page 270, Hadith 14174, from Zayd ibn al-Harith: "That Abu Bakr, when death approached him, sent for Umar to appoint him as successor. So the people said: 'Do you appoint over us Umar, a harsh and rough man? If he took charge of us, he would be even harsher and rougher!'" He is now harsh and rough, so how will it be if authority falls into his hands? "'Do you appoint over us Umar, a harsh and rough man? If he took charge of us, he would be even harsher and rougher. So what will you say to your Lord when you meet Him and you have appointed Umar over us?'" to the end of the speech.

This is the evaluation of the Companions, and this is the evaluation of Ali, which we read a short while ago in Nahj al-Balagha in the Shiqshiqiyyah Sermon. And this is the evaluation of the women to whom Umar ibn al-Khattab proposed. And this is the evaluation of a Bedouin selling camels! What does he say about Umar? This is Kanz al-Ummal, the fourth volume of Kanz al-Ummal by al-Muttaqi al-Hindi. The narration is from Anas ibn Malik: "That a Bedouin came with his camels to sell them. So Umar came to bargain with him. So Umar began to prod the camels, one by one..." Prod them, meaning he strikes them severely. "So Umar began to prod the camels, one by one, striking them with his foot to make the camel rise, to see how his heart or his legs are. So the Bedouin began to say: 'Leave my camels alone, may you have no father!' But Umar would not be stopped by the Bedouin's saying, from doing that to camel after camel. So the Bedouin said to Umar: **'Indeed, I think you are a man of evil (Rajul Su').'**" Or a man of evil. "When he finished with them, he bought them..." to the end of the speech.

And I mentioned this incident in the past episodes and programs, as I recall I mentioned it in the Fatimi File, and it has another detail. But my point is that the opinion of this Bedouin who was dealing with the Caliph in selling camels is this! He says to him: "Leave my camels alone, may you have no father!" Then he said to him: "Indeed, I think you are a man of evil."
Segment 5: More Beatings and the Forced Marriage to Atikah

Sheikh al-Ghizzi provides further historical texts confirming the Caliph's violent tendencies, such as beating people for fasting in the month of Rajab, beating a former apostate who asked for charity, and brutalizing his own sister and brother-in-law prior to his conversion. Most disturbing is the account from Al-Tabaqat al-Kubra by Ibn Sa'd detailing Umar's marriage to Atikah bint Zayd. Despite her explicit refusal, Umar commanded her guardian to marry her to him anyway. The text explicitly states that Umar entered upon her, physically wrestled and fought her until he overpowered her, and then forced himself upon her, only to express disgust immediately afterward. The Sheikh highlights this to contrast the Caliph's character with the noble, gentle character of the Prophet.

The issue does not stop at this limit. When we go to the eighth volume of Kanz al-Ummal, we read that Kharashah ibn al-Hurr said: **"I saw Umar ibn al-Khattab striking the palms of men for fasting in Rajab, until they placed them in the food,"** meaning he strikes them and forces them to eat! Hadith 2475, under the title "Fasting of Rajab". Knowing that fasting in Rajab is a recommended fast, and the narrations regarding it are reported from the paths of the Shia and from the paths of the Sunnis. And in the same book it mentions from Anas: "In Paradise there is a palace that no one enters except those who fast in Rajab." These narrations are present in the same chapter. "In Paradise there is a palace..." This is from the Musnad of Anas. From Amir ibn Shibl al-Harami, and he transmits from Anas ibn Malik: "In Paradise there is a palace that no one enters except those who fast in Rajab." And from Abu Qilabah: "In Paradise there is a palace for those who fast in Rajab." And the month of Rajab, as in our narrations, is the month of Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him; perhaps there is a connection to this matter. "I saw Umar ibn al-Khattab striking the palms of men for fasting in Rajab, until they placed them in the food." He says: "Rajab, and what is Rajab? Rajab is only a month that the pre-Islamic era used to venerate. So when Islam came, it was abandoned." He does not know the hadiths of the Messenger of Allah, and does not know the Sunnah of the Messenger of Allah. And how often he said when they would say to him, "Were you not with the Messenger of Allah?" He said: "The buying and selling in the markets distracted me from that." This word was always repeated by Caliph Umar in the days of his caliphate! People fast the recommended fast, the Caliph strikes their palms until they place their hands in the food! "I saw Umar ibn al-Khattab striking the palms of men for fasting in Rajab, until they placed them in the food," to the end of what came in the hadith.

This is Al-Durr al-Manthur. This is the eighth volume, according to the Dar Ihya' al-Turath al-Arabi edition. In the issue of the verses from Surah 'Abasa, "and fruits and Abba". From Anas, that Umar recited on the pulpit: "And We caused to grow within it grain, and grapes and pasture..." to His saying: "and Abba". He said: "All of this we have known, so what is the Abba?" Then he raised a cane that was in his hand—the cane is present always in every place! Then he raised a cane that was in his hand, and he said: "This, by the life of Allah, is over-burdening. So there is nothing upon you that you do not know what the Abba is. Follow what its guidance has been clarified to you from the Book, so act upon it. And what you do not know, leave it to its Lord." As if it were an issue... meaning from the secrets of the knowledge of Monotheism, so that it is entrusted to Allah the Glorified and Exalted, while it is the name of fodder! The same al-Suyuti transmits from Ibn Abbas that Nafi' ibn al-Azraq asked him, asked Ibn Abbas about His saying "and Abba". He said: "The Abba is what the animals graze on." He said: "And do the Arabs know that?" Nafi' here asks because he knows that Abu Bakr and Umar did not know the meaning of the Abba! Therefore he asks about this issue. "He said: 'And do the Arabs know that?' He said: 'Yes, did you not hear the saying of the poet: You see in it the Abba and the gourd mixed, upon the watering place beneath which flows the sweet water.'" Meaning this word is known among the Arabs. The point of evidence here is what al-Suyuti mentioned; he said after the chain of narration: "A man asked Umar about His saying 'and Abba'. So when he saw them saying..." meaning he saw the people sitting—this man asked Umar, and Umar is ignorant of the subject, so there was a group of people who started saying that the Abba means such and such. "So when he saw them saying, **he turned upon them with the whip!**" Also he struck them in every place. We notice that the whip, and these are the books of the people, these are not our books, we find that the beating is present in every situation from the situations.

Let us go to the book Al-Futuhat al-Islamiyyah by Ahmad ibn Zayni Dahlan, and this is the first volume, page 28, Dar Sader edition, page 28-29. In the course of his mentioning Banu Sulaym and what was mentioned about their apostasy. And among them was Abu Shajarah ibn Abd al-'Uzza al-Sulami, and he is the son of al-Khansa. And he had apostatized and said poetry, from it his saying: "I quenched my spear from the battalion of Khalid"—meaning Khalid ibn al-Walid—"And indeed I hope after it to live long, or to live long." He indicates that he killed Khalid and killed Umar ibn al-Khattab. "So when he became Muslim"—meaning he returned from his apostasy—"Abu Bakr, may Allah be pleased with him, accepted Islam from him. So when it was the caliphate of Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, he arrived at Medina"—this Abu Shajarah—"and he saw Umar dividing money among the poor. So he said: 'Give me, for I am in need.' So he said: 'And who are you?' So he said: 'Abu Shajarah ibn Abd al-'Uzza al-Sulami.' He said: 'O enemy of Allah! No, by Allah, are you not the one who says: I quenched my spear from the battalion of Khalid, and indeed I hope after it to live long?' **And Umar began to strike him with the whip on his head!** So he raced him running to his camel, rode it, and joined his people," and said verses to the end of the speech. My point is that the beating with the stick is in all directions. All these are examples through which a picture becomes clear to us, a picture of the character of the Caliph is drawn for us. This is Al-Futuhat al-Islamiyyah by Ahmad ibn Zayni Dahlan.

Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh by Ibn al-Athir, and this is the first volume of Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh, Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah edition, page 602-603. Umm Abdullah bint Abi Hathmah said, and she was the wife of Amir ibn Rabi'ah: "We were departing to the land of Abyssinia, and Amir—who is her husband—had gone for some of his needs, when Umar approached while he was upon his polytheism until he stood over me." She says: "And we used to face affliction, harm, and severity from him." Because he was in the groups of torturers who tortured the Muslims. Umm Abdullah bint Abi Hathmah said, and she was the wife of Amir ibn Rabi'ah: "We were departing to the land of Abyssinia, and Amir had gone for some of his needs, when Umar approached while he was upon his polytheism until he stood over me. And we used to face affliction, harm, and severity from him. So he said: 'Are you setting out, O Umm Abdullah?' She said: I said: 'Yes, by Allah, we will surely go out in the land of Allah, **for you have harmed us and oppressed us!** For you have harmed us and oppressed us, until Allah makes a way out for us.'" She said: "So he said: 'May Allah accompany you,' and I saw in him a tenderness and sadness. She said: So when Amir returned, I informed him and said to him: 'If you had seen Umar, and his tenderness and his sadness for us.' He said: 'Did you hope for his Islam?' I said: 'Yes.' **He said: 'He will not become Muslim until the donkey of al-Khattab becomes Muslim!'**" Due to what he used to see of his harshness and severity against the Muslims.

Then he mentions regarding the issue of the reason for Umar's Islam, the issue of his entering upon his sister. "Will you not return to your family?" this is the one addressing Umar ibn al-Khattab, "so you straighten their affair." He said: "And which of my family?" He said: "Your brother-in-law"—brother-in-law meaning your sister's husband—"your brother-in-law and your cousin Sa'id ibn Zayd, and your sister Fatima. For by Allah they have become Muslim." So Umar returned to them both, and Khabbab ibn al-Aratt was with them both, teaching them the Quran. So when they heard the movement of Umar, Khabbab hid, and Fatima took the scroll and placed it under her thighs. And Umar had heard the recitation of Khabbab. So when he entered, he said: "What is this murmuring?" meaning what is this sound I was hearing? He said: "You heard nothing." He said: "Yes, and I have been informed that you both have followed Muhammad upon his religion." **And he violently attacked his brother-in-law Sa'id ibn Zayd. So his sister stood up to him to stop him, so he struck her and cracked her head open!** Until he says: "And when Umar saw what was on his sister of blood," meaning he struck her a blow until he made her bleed. And therefore this speech comes mentioned in Al-Tabaqat al-Kubra by Ibn Sa'd. This is Al-Tabaqat al-Kubra, and this is the third and fourth volume, Dar Ihya' al-Turath al-Arabi, its indexes prepared by Riyad Abdullah Abd al-Hadi, first edition 1996, Dar Ihya' al-Turath al-Arabi, Beirut, Lebanon, page 142 from the third volume. He said: "So Umar walked grumbling"—grumbling, meaning complaining, angry—"until he came to them both, and with them was a man from the Muhajirun"—meaning with his sister and her husband—"called Khabbab." Until he says: "So his brother-in-law said to him: 'Do you see, O Umar, if the truth was in other than your religion?' He said: **So Umar leaped upon his brother-in-law, and trampled him a severe trampling! So his sister came and pushed him away from her husband, so he slapped her with his hand a slap that made her face bleed!**" to the end of the story. My point is that the issue of beating is an issue present in all directions, in the time of ignorance, in the time of Islam, before he was a Caliph, after he was a Caliph.

When we go to the second volume of Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh, we find the exact same speech which I read a short while ago in Tarikh al-Tabari. When he proposed to Umm Kulthum the daughter of Abu Bakr, she said: "He is harsh in living, severe upon women." And when he proposed to Umm Aban bint Utbah, she hated him and said: "He locks his door, prevents his good, enters frowning, and exits frowning." And on page 461, the exact same speech which came mentioned in the story of the Shura, and that he commanded the severing of the heads of the Companions who opposed what resulted from that council: "If five agree and one refuses, then crush his head with the sword. And if four agree and two refuse, then strike their heads. And if three agree on a man and three on a man, then make Abdullah ibn Umar the judge. If they do not agree to the judgment of Abdullah ibn Umar, then be with those among whom is Abdul Rahman ibn Awf, and kill the rest." The exact same contents and the exact same meanings are present whenever we leaf through the books of history that talk about Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab.

Also if we wanted to go with Al-Tabaqat al-Kubra, and this is the third volume, page 152-153: "That Umar ibn al-Khattab was brought wealth, so he began dividing it among the people. So they crowded upon him. So Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas approached crowding the people until he reached him. **So Umar struck him with the whip!**" Meaning there is no one except he is struck with the whip. "So Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas approached crowding the people until he reached him. So Umar struck him with the whip, and said: 'You approached not fearing the authority of Allah on the earth, so I wanted to teach you that the authority of Allah does not fear you.'" So he struck Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas! A short while ago we read the speech of Amr ibn al-Aas that they used to be in awe of him and fear him from his harshness and roughness, and these are his companions. And therefore, this roughness and harshness might reflect in some of the daily behaviors in everyday life. As, for example, for example, this incident: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari gifted... this is page 164. Abu Musa al-Ash'ari gifted the wife of Umar, Atikah bint Zayd ibn Amr ibn Nufayl, a small carpet (Tinfisah). I estimate it to be a cubit and a span, like a prayer rug. I estimate it to be a cubit and a span, meaning the length of this rug approximately, not precisely. Meaning like a prayer rug. Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, and Abu Musa is from his companions, gifted the wife of Umar, Atikah, a small carpet. I estimate it to be a cubit and a span. So Umar entered upon her, he saw it. "What is this?" She said: "Abu Musa al-Ash'ari gifted it to me." So he took it and struck her head with it! He struck his wife's head with it! Then he said: "Bring Abu Musa to me." So he was brought, he was made to run! He was made to run, meaning run, he was brought running and dragged! Then Ali said: "By Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, and exhaust him." He said: "So he was brought, exhausted, saying: 'Do not rush against me, O Commander of the Believers.' So Umar said: 'What made you gift to my women?' Then Umar took it and struck him with it over his head! And said: 'Take it, we have no need for it.'"

These atmospheres. Let us read this incident, page 105: "So al-Zubayr came"—I only take the points of need, otherwise there are many details from the same source, this is the third volume from Al-Tabaqat al-Kubra by Ibn Sa'd, and it is from the known books of the people. "So al-Zubayr came, and I was at the door"—this is Aslam, who was a servant to Umar ibn al-Khattab. Aslam said: "So al-Zubayr came, al-Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, and I was at the door, so he asked me to enter, to enter upon Umar. So I said: 'The Commander of the Believers is busy for an hour.' **So he raised his hand and struck behind my ear a blow that made me cry out!**" Beating in all directions! This is the atmosphere! Meaning al-Zubayr now came to meet Umar, Aslam is at the door, he said to him: "The Commander of the Believers is busy for an hour." So al-Zubayr grabbed the boy and hit him! Why is the issue in the hands of the boy? The boy is under orders! "So he raised his hand and struck behind my ear a blow that made me cry out. He said: So I entered upon Umar, so he said: 'What is the matter with you?' So I said: 'Al-Zubayr hit me,' and I told him his news. He said: So Umar began saying: 'Al-Zubayr, by Allah, I see...' Then he said: 'Let him in.' So he was let in to Umar, so Umar said: 'Why did you hit this boy?' So al-Zubayr said: 'He claimed that he would prevent me from entering upon you.' So Umar said: 'Has he ever turned you away from my door?' He said: 'No.' Umar said: 'So if he said wait an hour, for the Commander of the Believers is busy...'" to the end of the speech. Meaning it is this atmosphere, al-Zubayr also came and hit the boy, because the atmosphere is this existing one, an atmosphere of beating without reason and with no reason.

On page 170. It is these atmospheres, the atmospheres of Bedouinism, the atmospheres of cruelty. On page 170, with his chain of narration, that Umar used to wipe with his sandals, and say—meaning after he eats he would wipe with his sandals—and say: "The napkins of the family of Umar are their sandals." Also, Sa'id ibn Mansur informed us with his chain of narration from al-Sa'ib ibn Yazid. Al-Sa'ib says: "I might have had dinner at Umar ibn al-Khattab's, so he would eat bread and meat, then he would wipe his hand on his foot, then say: 'This is the napkin of Umar and the family of Umar.'" And the likes of this is abundant. This clarifies to us models of the nature of life, Bedouinism, and roughness. But I will turn away from it due to the lack of time, because we have other demands to point to.

In Al-Tabaqat al-Kubra, in the eighth volume of the same edition, page 382. The eighth volume, page 382: "Affan ibn Muslim informed us, Hammad ibn Salama narrated to us, Ali ibn Zayd informed us, that Atikah bint Zayd was married to Abdullah ibn Abi Bakr, and he died leaving her." This is page 382 from the eighth volume. "That Atikah bint Zayd was married to Abdullah ibn Abi Bakr, and he died leaving her. And he stipulated upon her that she not marry after him. So she devoted herself to worship and made herself celibate. She devoted herself to worship, she made herself celibate (not to marry). And men began to propose to her, and she kept refusing. So Umar said to her guardian: 'Mention me to her.' So he mentioned him to her. But she refused Umar too. **So Umar said: 'Marry her to me!'** Even if she was not pleased. So Umar said: 'Marry her to me.' So he married her to him. So Umar came to her, and entered upon her, and wrestled her until he overpowered her over herself, and consummated the marriage with her (Nakahaha). When he finished, he said: 'Uff, uff, uff! Disgust for her!' Then he went out from her and left her, and did not come to her."

I here do not want to talk about the issue that this is not a lawful marriage. I do not want to enter into this demand, even though this contract is called in jurisprudence an "unauthorized contract" (Aqd Fuduli). And this issue is known, perhaps at the end of the incident she agreed. "Then he went out from her and left her, and did not come to her. So she sent to him a freedwoman of hers saying, 'Come, for I will prepare myself for you after that.'" The unauthorized contract, if the person concerned agrees, the contract becomes valid. I do not want to enter into the issue of discussing the lawful or unlawful contract. **I am discussing the moral issue in dealing!** Even if she had agreed before what happened to her happened, should the treatment be in this way? My talk is about roughness and harshness. **"So he said to her guardian: 'Mention me to her.'"** He said to him: mention me to her. So he mentioned him to her. But she refused Umar too, she was refusing. "So Umar said: 'Marry her to me!'" Even though she was refusing. "So he married her to him. So Umar came to her, and entered upon her, and wrestled her until he overpowered her over herself, and consummated the marriage with her." My talk here is about this morality, about this nature. And you notice, these are not Shiite books, these are the books of the people: books of history, books of biographies. If the books of history are weak, what are the books of biographies? If the books of biographies are weak, what are the books of hadith? If the books of hadith are weak, what are the books of exegesis? All these books agree upon these truths.
Segment 6: Beating His Wife and Attacking the Houses of the Prophet's Widows

Sheikh al-Ghizzi shares further accounts from canonical Sunni sources demonstrating the Caliph's violent disposition. He cites Sunan Ibn Majah where Umar wakes up in the middle of the night to beat his wife, later justifying it with a fabricated hadith: "A man is not asked why he beats his wife." The Sheikh then details multiple shocking incidents from Tarikh al-Tabari and Kanz al-Ummal wherein Umar repeatedly invades the homes of the Prophet's widows—Aisha and Maymunah. Angered by women mourning the deaths of Abu Bakr and Khalid ibn al-Walid, Umar ignores the sanctity of the Prophet's houses, orders his guards to drag the women out into the street, and personally beats them with his whip one by one, even striking them until their head-coverings fall off, callously declaring, "They have no sanctity!"

When we go to Sunan Ibn Majah, and this is the second volume of Sunan Ibn Majah, Dar al-Kutub al-Ilmiyyah edition, verified by Mahmoud Muhammad Mahmoud Hasan Nassar, second edition 2009. Sunan Ibn Majah page 485, Hadith 1986, Chapter of Beating Women. With his chain of narration from Abdul Rahman al-Musallami, from al-Ash'ath ibn Qays. He said: "I was a guest of Umar one night. So when it was the middle of the night, he stood up to his wife and beat her!" The middle of the night. "So I intervened between them." And what appears is that Umar was sleeping near al-Ash'ath, and in the middle of the night he woke up and went and beat his wife! "I was a guest of Umar one night. So when it was the middle of the night, he stood up to his wife and beat her. So I intervened between them. When he returned to his bed, he said to me: 'O Ash'ath, memorize from me something I heard from the Messenger of Allah:
A man is not asked about why he beats his wife. And do not sleep except upon a Witr (odd-numbered prayer).
And I forgot the third.'"

This incident is mentioned in the book of Al-Sunan, Sunan Abi Dawud. And this is the second volume, Dar Ihya' al-Turath al-Arabi edition, verified by Muhammad Adnan bin Yasin Darwish, first edition year 2000 AD, 1421 Hijri, page 357, Hadith 2147. With the exact same chain of narration, from al-Ash'ath ibn Qays, from Umar ibn al-Khattab, from the Prophet, he said: "A man is not asked about why he beat his wife." **The hadith in its entirety was chewed up (censored)! Nothing remained of it except this phrase:** "A man is not asked about why he beat his wife." And this is an example of the examples of chewing up the hadiths, and distortion, and cutting, and it is very clear. And this is from the authentic books (Sihah) of the people, the book of Al-Sunan, Sunan Abi Dawud. In the middle of the night he wakes up and beats his wife, then he returns and brings this hadith from the Messenger of Allah: "A man is not asked about why he beats his wife."

The issue does not stop at this limit! Just as he entered upon Atikah, wrestled her, and consummated the marriage with her by force, here he beats his wife. Here he beats the Companions. There he beats a man who fasted it (Rajab). There he beats the palms for fasting the month of Rajab until they put their hands in the food. And he beats a people who explained the verse "and fruits and Abba". And the issue continues in this manner! And it passed upon us how he beat his son because he combed his hair and wore beautiful clothes. Some might say this is a lying incident, that is a lying incident. But what about these dozens and dozens and dozens of incidents, are they all lying? This is a nature. The complaint of the Companions to Abu Bakr while they say to him, "What will you say to Allah while you appoint Umar over us, this harsh and rough man?" What will you say to Allah? The opinions of women in his time... all of this reveals the character that Umar ibn al-Khattab was upon.
The coming episode tomorrow, God willing, which is the sixth episode, we will see how Umar ibn al-Khattab deals with the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family.

This is Tarikh al-Tabari. And this is the second volume of Tarikh al-Tabari, Dar Sader edition, verified by Nawwaf al-Jarrah, page 589. With his chain of narration: "Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib narrated to me, he said: When Abu Bakr, may Allah have mercy on him, died, Aisha established mourning for him. So Umar ibn al-Khattab approached until he stood at her door." At the door of Aisha. "When Abu Bakr, may Allah have mercy on him, died, Aisha established mourning for him. So Umar ibn al-Khattab approached until he stood at her door, and forbade them from crying over Abu Bakr. But they refused to stop. So Umar said to Hisham ibn al-Walid"—this was a henchman (Jilwaz) for Umar ibn al-Khattab—"So Umar said to Hisham ibn al-Walid: 'Enter and bring out to me the daughter of Abu Quhafa!'" "Enter and bring out to me the daughter of Abu Quhafa"—the sister of Abu Bakr, and she is Umm Farwah. "So Aisha said to Hisham when she heard that from Umar: **'I make my house forbidden (Aharriju) upon you!'**" I do not permit you to enter my house! "So Umar said to Hisham: 'Enter, for I have permitted you.' I am the one who permitted you! **So Hisham entered into the middle of the women, and brought out Umm Farwah, the sister of Abu Bakr, to Umar. So he struck her with the whip, he beat her blows!** So the mourners scattered when they heard that."

Meaning this is an attack on the house of Aisha, and Aisha made entering forbidden upon them. He said: "Enter and bring her out from among the women!" He brought her out to the street, because Umar was not inside the house. "So Umar said to Hisham ibn al-Walid: 'Enter and bring out to me the daughter of Abu Quhafa, the sister of Abu Bakr.' So Aisha said to Hisham when she heard that from Umar: 'I make my house forbidden upon you!' So Umar said to Hisham: 'Enter, for I have permitted you.' So Hisham entered and brought out Umm Farwah to the street, the sister of Abu Bakr, to Umar. So he struck her with the whip, he beat her blows! So the mourners scattered when they heard that." And this incident is repeated and echoed not in one source, but in very many sources.

Other models, and this is Kanz al-Ummal. This is Kanz al-Ummal, and it is the fifteenth volume of Kanz al-Ummal. From Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib, he said: "When Abu Bakr died..." page 309, Hadith 42902. "When Abu Bakr died, Aisha established mourning for him. So it reached Umar, and he forbade her from mourning over Abu Bakr, but they refused to stop. So he said to Hisham ibn al-Walid: 'Bring out to me the daughter of Abu Quhafa!' **So he struck her with the whip, blows.**" According to the context, the speech is about Aisha here. And perhaps this narration is the exact same narration that al-Tabari mentioned, and the speech is about Umm Farwah, but according to the context, the talk is about Aisha, and Umm Farwah is not mentioned at all in this issue. So what appears is that the beating was upon Aisha. "When Abu Bakr died, Aisha established mourning for him. So it reached Umar, and he forbade her from mourning over Abu Bakr, but they refused to stop. So he said to Hisham ibn al-Walid: 'Bring out to me the daughter of Abu Quhafa!' So definitely the intended here is Aisha. 'So he struck her with the whip, blows. So the mourning women scattered when they heard that,'" to the end of what came in the hadith. Meaning also here is an attack on the house of the Prophet's wife.

Hadith 42904, from Sa'id ibn al-Musayyib, he said: "When Abu Bakr died, he was wept over. So Umar said: 'The Messenger of Allah said: Indeed the dead person is punished by the crying of the living.' But they refused except to cry—the family of Abu Bakr refused except to cry. So Umar said to Hisham ibn al-Walid: 'Stand and bring out the women.'" This is more than one picture, more than one incident. Because once he said to him, "Bring out Umm Farwah, the sister of Abu Bakr," and he beat her in the street, he brought her outside the house. And once he brought out Aisha, as appears from the previous text, and beat Aisha. Now another picture: "Stand and bring out the women." "So Aisha said: 'I forbid you.' So Umar said: 'Enter.'" "I forbid you," meaning I make entering forbidden upon you, O Hisham. "So Umar said: 'Enter, for I have permitted you.' So he entered. So Aisha said: **'Are you going to bring me out, O my little son?'**" Because previously he had brought her out and beaten her, the previous hadith that passed: "He said to Hisham ibn al-Walid, 'Bring out to me the daughter of Abu Quhafa,' so he struck her with the whip, blows." This beating fell upon Aisha. Now a second time: "Stand and bring out the women. So Aisha said: 'I forbid you.' So Umar said: 'Enter, for I have permitted you.' So he entered. So Aisha said: 'Are you going to bring me out, O my little son?' He said: 'As for you, I have permitted you.' **So he began to bring them out, woman by woman, while he beats them with the whip! Until Umm Farwah came out, and Umm Farwah came out, and Umm Farwah was beaten!**"

As passed, it seems there is more than one time he attacks the house of Aisha and beats. Once he requests from Hisham, as in Tarikh al-Tabari, to bring out for him Umm Farwah, the sister of Abu Bakr. And once he wants Aisha. And another time he wants all the women, and the women line up in a row and he beats them one by one! "So he began to bring them out, woman by woman, while he beats them with the whip!" This is Kanz al-Ummal, Hadith 42904, page 309.

Hadith 42899, from Nasr ibn Abi Asim: "That Umar heard a mourning woman—or a mourning group—in Medina at night. And both readings are correct: a mourning woman or a mourning group. That Umar heard a mourning woman in Medina at night. **So he came to her, and entered upon her, and scattered the women, and caught the mourning woman!**" "Scattered the women," meaning he entered without permission! He heard mourning, so he entered without permission, as was his habit to enter houses without permission, as appears from the events. "That Umar heard a mourning woman in Medina at night. So he came to her, and entered upon her, and scattered the women, and caught the mourning woman." The mourning woman almost fled, but he caught her. **"So he began to strike her with the whip, so her head-covering (Khimar) fell off! So they said: 'Her hair, O Commander of the Believers!' So he said: 'Indeed, I know. She has no sanctity!'"** There is no sanctity for her hair!

Hadith 42908, from Amr ibn Dinar, page 308. Books, the books of the people, and the sources are the sources of the people, and the chains of narration are the chains of narration of the people, and the hadiths are the hadiths of the people! "When Khalid ibn al-Walid died, women gathered in the house of Maymunah..." Maymunah is from the wives of the Prophet, one of the Mothers of the Believers. "...women gathered in the house of Maymunah crying. **So Umar came, and Ibn Abbas was with him, and he had the whip. So he said: 'O Abdullah, enter upon the Mother of the Believers, upon Maymunah, and command her to veil herself, and bring them out to me!' So he began to bring them out to him while he strikes them with the whip, one by one!**" Once he attacks the house of Aisha, once he attacks the house of Maymunah. "So he began to bring them out to him while he strikes them with the whip, one by one. So the head-covering of a woman among them fell off. So they said: 'O Commander of the Believers, her head-covering!' So he said: 'Leave her, for she has no sanctity.'" And the Companions or the people used to marvel at his saying, how could he say she has no sanctity?

These are pictures and clips from the biography and from the life of Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab. And the likes of this is very much if we wanted to trace what came in the books of history, what came in the books of biographies, what came in the books of hadith. But I believe that with these models, I do not want to prolong upon you much. And it was possible for me to bring more sources than this, but I find in that a cause for boredom. Otherwise, there are very many incidents present in the books: whether in the books of history, in the books of biographies, in the books of Rijal, in the books of hadith, in the books of exegesis, in all types and categories of books among the people, the likes of these incidents are present. Beating for what reason?

Therefore, the Commander of the Believers, as I read to your hearing a short while ago in the Shiqshiqiyyah Sermon, what does he say? **"So he made it in a harsh enclosure, where its speech is blunt, and its touch is rough, and stumbles are many in it,"** to the end of his words, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him.
Segment 7: The Ethics of the Prophet and the Pedigree of Ali

Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes the episode by directly contrasting the documented harshness of Umar with the sublime, gentle character of Prophet Muhammad, as described in the Quran and hadith. He quotes from Bihar al-Anwar showing the Prophet's immense humility, patience, and kindness—even when accosted by a rough Bedouin. Finally, the Sheikh recites from the Qasi'ah sermon in Nahj al-Balagha, illustrating how Imam Ali was personally raised and polished by the Prophet from childhood. Because Imam Ali was the true, flawless reflection of the Prophet's ethics, he is the only one fit to be his rightful successor.

These are the ethics of Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab. I do not want to say that everything mentioned about Umar ibn al-Khattab is of this manner, never. That would be a distortion of the facts. Many good things have been mentioned about Umar ibn al-Khattab, present in the books of history. But I want to say here... perhaps a sayer might say: "So why did you not mention these good things?" I want to point to an issue here: there is no human except that he has good deeds and bad deeds. I have no problem with Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab; the issue is a doctrinal issue. When I talk about a caliph for the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, he must be the most resembling of people to the Messenger of Allah! And the first thing looked at is the character. **This character, and this nature, does it resemble the character of the Messenger of Allah?** I am not responsible for the end of Caliph Umar, or the end of anyone; every human is responsible for his end. The talk is about this subject: the talk is that the one who is a caliph for the Messenger of Allah... there must be some resemblance between him and the Messenger of Allah. I do not say in the scientific aspect, and I do not say in the lineage aspect, and I do not say in the aspect of purity and infallibility; at least in the moral aspect, in dealing with the people!

These ethics which I talked about, all these witnesses, all these books... is there between them and the ethics of the Messenger of Allah anything of resemblance? Can anyone say that? Let us see what the Noble Quran tells us about the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family.
The Noble Quran tells us in Surah al-Tawbah, in the 128th verse:
There has certainly come to you a Messenger from among yourselves. Grievous to him is what you suffer; [he is] concerned over you and to the believers is kind and merciful.

Grievous to him is what you suffer, it pains him that harm, fatigue, pain, and hardship afflict you. "Grievous to him is what you suffer," it pains him that fatigue, pain, and illness afflict you. "Concerned over you," concerned over your lives, over your future, over your ends. "To the believers is kind and merciful."
And likewise in the 159th verse of Surah Al Imran:
So by mercy from Allah, you were lenient with them. And if you had been rude [in speech] and harsh in heart, they would have disbanded from about you.

"So pardon them and ask forgiveness for them and consult them in the matter. And when you have decided, then rely upon Allah. Indeed, Allah loves those who rely [upon Him]." "So by mercy from Allah, you were lenient with them. And if you had been rude and harsh in heart..." These ethics are entirely contradictory to what passed upon us from the witnesses and from the historical events whose mention passed!
And the verse in Surah al-Qalam:
And indeed, you are of a great moral character.

This verse is vaster and greater than all the meanings that came mentioned in these noble verses. Thus the Quran tells us about the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family. These are his ethics, and these are his traits, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family.

In a side of the hadiths that talked about some of the traits of the Messenger of Allah... This is the sixteenth volume of the book Bihar al-Anwar. From the Master of Legatees, while he is talking about an aspect, I mention lines, otherwise the narrations are long and detailed. The Commander of the Believers says:

The Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, never shook hands with anyone and then withdrew his hand from his hand, until the man was the one who withdrew his hand. And no one ever conversed with him regarding a need or a hadith, and then left, until the man was the one who left. And no one disputed with him in speech until the man was the one who went silent. And he was never seen stretching his leg in front of someone sitting with him, ever.
These are the ethics of the Messenger of Allah, an aspect of them. These are traces, these are sides of his ethics and of his dealing, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family.

In another place, from Anas ibn Malik, who was a servant to the Messenger of Allah, he said: "I accompanied the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, for ten years. And I smelled all perfumes, but I did not smell a scent sweeter than his scent." And when he spoke, the sweet scent would emanate from the sides of his noble mouth. "And if someone from his companions met him, he would stand with him and not leave until the man was the one who left him. And if someone from his companions met him, he would take his hand and hand it to him, and not let go of it until the man was the one who let go of it." And he never pushed his knees out in front of someone sitting with him, ever. And no man ever sat with the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, and then the Prophet stood up until the man stood up.

And from him also, from Anas ibn Malik: "That a Bedouin caught up with the Prophet, and grabbed him by his cloak and pulled him a severe pull! Until I looked at the side of the neck of the Messenger of Allah, and the edge of the cloak had left a mark on it from the severity of his pull. Then he said to him: O Muhammad!—after he pulled the Prophet a severe pull—O Muhammad, order for me from the wealth of Allah that you have! **So the Messenger of Allah turned to him and laughed, and ordered a gift for him.**"

From Abu Sa'id al-Khudri: "The Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, was more bashful than the virgin in her secluded room." And from Ibn Abbas, from the Prophet, he said:
I am the well-mannered of Allah, and Ali is my well-mannered one (Ana Adib Allah wa Ali Adibi). My Lord commanded me with generosity and righteousness, and forbade me from stinginess and harshness.

And there is nothing more hated to Allah the Mighty and Majestic than stinginess and bad character. These are the words of the Greatest Prophet: "I am the well-mannered of Allah, and Ali is my well-mannered one. My Lord commanded me with generosity and righteousness, and forbade me from stinginess and harshness. And there is nothing more hated to Allah the Mighty and Majestic than stinginess and bad character. And indeed, bad character spoils the deed just as dirt spoils honey." Just as dirt spoils honey, bad character spoils the deed of a human.
This is an aspect of what came in the hadith about the descriptions of the Seal Prophet, and about his ethics, and about his traits, and his pure, purified characteristics. As he said:
My Lord disciplined me, so He perfected my discipline. And Ali is my disciplined one.

A word by the Christian writer George Jordac, a very beautiful word. He says: "Ali is a jewel created by Allah and crafted by Muhammad." And it is a translation for a side of the meaning of this hadith: "My Lord disciplined me, so He perfected my discipline. I am the disciplined of Allah, and Ali is my disciplined one."

Therefore, the Master of Legatees in Nahj al-Balagha, in his noble sermon in which he talks about his relationship with the Messenger of Allah, and it is the Qasi'ah Sermon numbered 192 in Nahj al-Balagha, he says in it:
And you have known my position with the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family.

These are the ethics of Ali! When we say that Ali is the best, just as the talk passed in the topic of knowledge, in the topic of courage, in the topic of generosity; the talk today is in the topic of ethics.

And you have known my position with the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, by close kinship and special status. He placed me in his lap while I was a child, pressing me to his chest

—for the Messenger of Allah raised him since his childhood—"He placed me in his lap while I was a child, pressing me to his chest, and enclosing me in his bed, and making me touch his body, and making me smell his scent. And he used to chew the thing and then feed it to me. **And he did not find in me a lie in speech, nor a mistake in action.**" And this is infallibility! "And he did not find in me a lie in speech, nor a mistake in action."

And Allah had joined with him, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, from the time he was weaned, the greatest angel of His angels, making him travel the path of noble traits and the beauties of the ethics of the world, night and day. And I used to follow him like a weanling following its mother!

The weanling (Fasil) is the baby camel. "I used to follow him like a weanling following its mother. He would raise for me every day a banner from his ethics, and command me to emulate it. And he used to reside every year in Hira, so I would see him and no one else would see him. And a single house did not gather anyone on that day in Islam other than the Messenger of Allah, Khadija, and I was their third. I see the light of revelation and the message, and I smell the scent of prophethood."

And I heard the groan of Satan when the revelation descended upon him. So I said: O Messenger of Allah, what is this groan? He said: This is Satan who has despaired of being worshipped. Indeed you hear what I hear, and you see what I see, except that you are not a prophet.

This is Ali, the well-mannered of the Messenger of Allah! And these are his ethics. The Quran told us about the ethics of the Messenger of Allah, the hadiths of the Ahlulbayt, and the noble Nahj al-Balagha, and the narrations of the Prophet, and the hadiths of the Prophet, and his words, give us a picture of the ethics of Ali. And these books which I read to your hearing fragments of, otherwise there is much, much more of the likes of these witnesses.

I do not judge anything. I leave the judgment to whoever possesses fairness. I leave the judgment to whoever possesses something of intellect and of wisdom. I do not deny what is in the books also of the good deeds of Caliph Umar, never. **But these ethics, are they the ethics of the Messenger of Allah? And whoever is not characterized by the ethics of the Messenger of Allah, does he have the right to sit in the place of the Messenger of Allah?** The issue needs study and research, and there are a thousand question marks and question marks. As for Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, his pages are clear, his ethics, his signs, his clear proofs. And everything related to Ali, the meanings of infallibility manifest in it, the meaning of knowledge manifests in it, the meaning of generosity manifests in it... and the ethics of the Messenger of Allah manifest in it, in all its dimensions and in all its meanings. "You have gathered, O Ali, in your traits the opposites, therefore peers became rare for you."

Prayers of Allah be upon you, O Master of Legatees, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings. Our appointment is renewed upon the love of Ali and the family of Ali. I will meet you, God willing, tomorrow in the sixth episode of the episodes of the Alawi File. I ask you for supplication. In the safety of Allah.

title: The File of Ali - Episode 6: The Burying of Truths
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 6

This episode examines the **systematic concealment and distortion** of historical facts concerning Imam Ali, a process the Sheikh terms the "burying of truths." He argues that since the day of Saqifah, religion has been made a **servant to politics**, leading to a widespread campaign to obscure Ali’s virtues in major Sunni works. The Sheikh scrutinizes Caliph Umar’s **blatant lack of etiquette** toward the Prophet, citing incidents from **Sahih al-Bukhari** where he physically restrained the Prophet and dismissed his final commands as **delirium**. Further, he exposes the intentional **erasure of Ali's name** in narratives transmitted by Lady Aisha and contrasts these fragmented reports with the unedited truths found in the **Book of Sulaym ibn Qays**. The episode concludes by highlighting the cosmic significance of loving Ali, described by the Prophet as the **human embodiment of Surah al-Tawhid** and the ultimate touchstone for one's legitimate lineage.
Segment 1: The Law of Burying Truths

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens the sixth episode by addressing a fundamental law of history: the deliberate burying and distortion of truths by political powers. He emphasizes that since the day of Saqifah, religion was made a servant to politics rather than politics serving religion. This resulted in a systematic campaign to bury the virtues of Ali and the Ahlulbayt, either by complete omission or by twisting facts to obscure their true meaning. The Sheikh notes that while uncovering these buried truths is difficult, scattered remnants still exist within the books of the opponents, which he intends to use to continue examining the "ethics" of Caliph Umar, specifically his behavior towards the Prophet.

when the Prophet wanted to pray over Abdullah ibn Ubayy, this is by the transmission of Sahih al-Bukhari. And al-Bukhari usually, when he transmits, tries to soften in his transmission of the narration or shorten the narration and condense the narration.

Did Allah not forbid you from praying over the hypocrites?

He pulled the Prophet and rebuked the Prophet: "Did Allah not forbid you from praying over the hypocrites?" He said: "I am between two choices," to the end of the narration. This is by the transmission of Sahih al-Bukhari. "So when he wanted to pray over him, Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, pulled him, and said: 'Did Allah not forbid you from praying over the hypocrites?'" **Bad manners, a complete ignorance, a compounded ignorance! And he wants to teach the Prophet, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family!** And harshness, and cruelty. And another time, while the Prophet is conversing with them and preaching to them, he raises the branch and strikes the earth with it in front of the Prophet, so the Busr scatters toward the face of the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family!** And these are a few models of what came in the books of the people in the books of history, otherwise much of the truths have gone, have been distorted, have been deformed.
Segment 3: The Raising of Voices Above the Prophet

Sheikh al-Ghizzi analyzes the opening verses of Surah al-Hujurat, which strictly forbid raising one's voice above the Prophet or speaking to him disrespectfully. While the Quranic verses are clear, the Sheikh shows how Sahih al-Bukhari and Tafsir al-Tabari confirm that these verses were revealed explicitly because Abu Bakr and Umar were shouting at each other in the Prophet's presence, ignoring basic etiquette and prophetic reverence. Despite attempts by Sunni compilers to soften the narrative or insert disconnected narrations, the core truth remains: the Caliphs lacked the fundamental manners required when in the presence of the Messenger of Allah.
The exact same thing if we wanted to go to Sahih al-Bukhari. In the Book of the Exegesis of the Quran from Sahih al-Bukhari, and regarding what relates to Surah al-Hujurat. Surah al-Hujurat:
O you who have believed, do not put [yourselves] before Allah and His Messenger but fear Allah. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing.
"Do not put [yourselves] before Allah and His Messenger," meaning do not suggest proposals in the affairs of the nation without the Messenger of Allah asking you, for that is bad manners!
O you who have believed, do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet.

These are issues of manners known to every human who has been raised in a polite society. People today, when they deal with a respected person, it is not needed to set laws and say to them: do not say this, do not say that, and do not do this. There are laws, manners, and customs known in society. Let us read what the verses say: "O you who have believed, do not put [yourselves] before Allah and His Messenger but fear Allah. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing. O you who have believed, do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet or be loud to him in speech like the loudness of some of you to others, lest your deeds become worthless while you perceive not. "Those who lower," meaning those who do not lower, Allah has not tested their hearts for righteousness, and there is no forgiveness for them, and no great reward. "Indeed, those who lower their voices before the Messenger of Allah - they are the ones whose hearts Allah has tested for righteousness. For them is forgiveness and great reward."
Indeed, those who call you from behind the chambers - most of them do not use reason. And if they had been patient until you [could] come out to them, it would have been better for them.

These are general manners. This is the society of the Companions in which the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, was living. Because these are general manners. Raising the voice over a respected person, over an elder person, is from bad manners, from bad character. Calling out from behind the chambers is from bad manners. "O you who have believed, do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet or be loud to him in speech like the loudness of some of you to others, lest your deeds become worthless while you perceive not."

Segment 6: Ali and Surah Al-Tawhid

Sheikh al-Ghizzi explores the profound connection between Imam Ali and Surah Al-Tawhid (Surah Al-Ikhlas). Just as reciting the Surah once, twice, or thrice equals a third, two-thirds, or the entire Quran, loving Ali with the heart equals a third of faith, adding the tongue equals two-thirds, and acting with the hand completes one's faith. The Sheikh also recounts the famous story of Salman al-Farsi, who truthfully claimed to recite the entire Quran daily simply by reciting Surah Al-Ikhlas three times.

Why does the speech here talk about the relationship of Ali with Surah al-Tawhid? I pause here for a bit, for the speech about Ali delights me, and I believe that the speech about Ali delights every pure heart searching for the truth.

Ali and Surah al-Tawhid. Segment 7: The Deathbed of the Prophet and the Burial of Truth

The Sheikh notes that even Caliph Umar directed Ka'b al-Ahbar to ask Ali—not Aisha—about the Prophet's final moments. He concludes the segment by observing the approaching end of the Hijri year, urging believers to board the "Ship of Hussein."

And this issue of hiding the name of Ali is not only in these places and witnesses and referents which I mentioned to you from Sahih al-Bukhari and from Sahih Muslim upon the tongue of Aisha or the tongue of others. There are very many witnesses! For example, also, this is Sahih al-Bukhari. You notice I did not close the book, because I extract all the hadiths for you from al-Bukhari. The first chapter, Chapter of Wills and the saying of the Prophet: "The will of a man is written with him." According to this edition, Hadith 2741.

With his chain of narration from al-Aswad, he said: "They mentioned in the presence of Aisha that Ali, may Allah be pleased with them both, was a legatee (Wasi)." May Allah be pleased with them both, meaning Aisha and Ali. "They mentioned in the presence of Aisha that Ali, may Allah be pleased with them both, was a legatee." Meaning Aisha here points to two matters. The first matter, she says: "When did the Prophet make a will to Ali? For the Prophet died upon my chest." Thus she says in this hadith. Meaning here she talked about two matters: that the Prophet died on her chest, and the second matter, when did he make a will to Ali? As if the will can only be at the moment of death! Aisha the Mother of the Believers, and the two witnesses are al-Bukhari and Muslim. This is Muslim, page 619. And the preservers among the companions of Muhammad, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, have known that I never replied to Allah nor to His Messenger for a single hour, ever!

We are all ships of salvation, but the Ship of Hussein is faster. And we are all doors of salvation, and the Door of Hussein is wider.
