Sheikh al-Ghizzi elaborates on the "Conscious Reading." He explains that its methodology is to know "Husayn who is Husayn," relying on deducing the essence by the essence. He illustrates this with a story of the poet Abu Nuwas. He then explains that just as a scientist needs a microscope, a person needs a specific spiritual instrument to read Husayn, describing him as the most complete genesis of divine beauty.
The methodology of the conscious reading is this: Husayn who is Husayn. And it is **deducing the essence by the essence.**

The poet Abu Nuwas one day in Tus, where our Imam Al-Ridha was there. Abu Nuwas went out of his house and a person passed adjacent to him. Abu Nuwas was not able to look at his face, but he felt that this person was Ali bin Musa Al-Ridha by the **feeling of the heart, not by traces and signs.** So he asked the people, "Who is this person?" They said, "He is Ali bin Musa Al-Ridha." So he answered spontaneously:
If the eye sees you from after a limit, and doubt opposes it regarding you, the heart affirms you.

This is the **knowledge of the hearts.** We try to read Husayn. If we want to read him, we are in need of a probe, to a microscope, in need of a binocular. Just as the chemist and the physicist in his laboratory are incapable of dealing with things with the bare eye, needing an instrument, so I can read Husayn with this type of reading.
Husayn, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, is the **most complete genesis of divine beauty** in this world that we live in.
Segment 6: Husayn as the Manifestation of True Beauty

Sheikh al-Ghizzi delves into esoteric theology, describing Husayn as the ultimate manifestation of divine beauty. He quotes narrations stating that Paradise and the Throne were created from the light of Husayn. He explains that these are profound "signs" (Isharat) meant for the spiritual elite, representing the divine beauty manifesting in every aspect of existence through Husayn.

When I say that Husayn is the most complete genesis of divine beauty, you feel this meaning in the signs in the words of the Prophet and his pure family. The Prophet speaks to us about the **substance of the holy emanation** from which beings were created, and he speaks about the opening of the Husayni light. And from this light which was opened, Allah created Paradise and the Houri with wide lovely eyes, and those are **signs and allusions.**

Hasan and Husayn are the Throne and the earring and the proximity. There is a group of narrations that speak to us that Allah adorned His Throne with Hasan and Husayn. These are symbols and signs. These are meanings further than the world of dust. Allah adorned the pillars of Paradise with Hasan and Husayn so it swayed just as the bride sways in joy.

As our Imam Al-Sadiq said: **Indeed the Quran descended upon four things, upon the expression and the sign and the subtleties and the realities.** The expression is for the commoners and the sign is for the elites. Their speech is like their Quran. What I mentioned of narrations are signs and allusions to the meanings of beauty that manifest in the Husayni reality.

And when I speak about beauty, I am not referring only to the sensory beauty. The highest ranking beauty is the **true beauty**. True beauty is perceived by intuition and is perceived by conscience without definition. **Beauty in its reality is this emotion by which the soul is moved.** He is the all-encompassing sign in the universe.
It is not the love of the dwellings that enamored my heart but the love of the one who inhabited the dwellings. The one who manifested in all sides of this existence.
Segment 7: The Aesthetic Genesis and the Innate Emotional Reaction

Sheikh al-Ghizzi asserts that the emotional reaction toward Husayn is an innate response to ultimate beauty, which exists independently of the tragic calamity. This intense emotional resonance is what elevated his companions. He then introduces Muslim bin Aqeel as a prime example of a heart captivated by this Husayni beauty.

Husayn is the **most beautiful of beauty** in the first ranks of existence. A narration transmitted by Sheikh Al-Saduq tells of the moment Husayn was born. Safiyyah bint Abdul-Muttalib says the Prophet said, "O aunt, bring me my son." I said, "O Messenger of Allah, we have not cleaned him yet." What did the Messenger of Allah say?
O aunt, you will clean him? Indeed Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, has cleaned him and purified him.

These signs are signs to this exact meaning—the meaning of beauty. Therefore, the hearts emotionally react with Husayn. Why? This **emotional reaction** is not only because of the calamity. **Even if the calamity had not occurred, the hearts would emotionally react with Husayn.** The calamity occurred because the hearts inherently emotionally react with Husayn, so that the giant project of Ashura becomes complete.

This emotional reaction is the innate being affected by beauty. Therefore, the companions of Husayn reached their rank by the **intensity of the emotional reaction** of their hearts. There are hearts that emotionally reacted to the Husayni beauty, so they had this status.

**Muslim bin Aqeel.** This night, the gatherings and the processions in Karbala renew the memory of Muslim bin Aqeel. Muslim bin Aqeel is included in this description from the widest doors. This Hashemite heart emotionally reacting to Husayn, Muslim bin Aqeel, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him.
Segment 8: The Bravery and Martyrdom of Muslim bin Aqeel

Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes the lecture by detailing the profound bravery and tragic martyrdom of Muslim bin Aqeel in Kufa. He highlights Muslim's solitary stand against hundreds of soldiers and recounts his final moments where he was wounded and denied a drink of water, departing the world as a guarantor of the tragedy of Husayn.

Muslim bin Aqeel was among the people who most resembled Amir al-Mu'minin. He was a lion from the lions of the family of Abu Talib. In the event of Siffin, the Master of Legatees placed him on the right flank of the army. He was following the footsteps of the Master of Legatees, Imam Al-Mujtaba, and the Master of Martyrs.

When Muslim spent his night in the house of Taw'ah, he saw Amir al-Mu'minin in a dream, saying to him: "O Muslim, the haste, the haste, for you are today with us." Muslim's last action as he bid farewell to the world is that he turned towards Husayn and visited Husayn. Ibn Ziyad sent five hundred soldiers for one man in a small house among the alleys.

In this moment, when they attacked him, he would take a man by his belt and throw him onto the houses. Ibn al-Ash'ath sought reinforcements, and he wrote to Ibn Ziyad: "O Emir, indeed you did not send me to a grocer from the grocers of Kufa... **Indeed you sent me to a sword from the swords of Muhammad bin Abdullah.**"

Swords and spears fell upon every part of his body until he became pieces of blood. At the gate of the palace, he asked for a drink of water. They poured for him the first pour. So when he brought it near his lips, the cup filled with fresh blood. The second time, it filled with fresh blood. The third time, his front teeth fell into the cup, so he did not drink. Thus he departed the world. A **guarantor of his blood**, guaranteeing the tragedy of Husayn.

O Lord of Husayn, by the right of Husayn, heal the chest of Husayn with the appearance of Al-Hujjah, peace be upon him. O Allah, make us live the life of Muhammad and the family of Muhammad. I ask you all for prayers. And our final call is that praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds, and may Allah bless our Master and Prophet Muhammad and his most pure, most clean family.

title: The File of Ashura - Episode 2: Uncovering the Truth and Preserving the Progeny
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Video Overview: Episode 2

In this second episode of **The File of Ashura**, Sheikh al-Ghizzi elaborates on the **short and medium-term objectives** of the giant strategic project of Karbala. He provides a devastating historical critique of the Ummah's post-prophetic coup, citing Sunni sources to document the systematic persecution of loyal companions like **Malik bin Nuwayrah**, **Abu Dharr Al-Ghifari**, and **Ammar bin Yasir**. The Sheikh identifies the Umayyad era as the birth of sophisticated **media warfare**, where the Roman-style propaganda of Muawiyah was used to drown the truth. He establishes that the **short-term objective** was to create a permanent separating line between truth and falsehood, while the **medium-term objective** used the **strategy of weeping** and emotional resonance to tether the hearts of the Shia to the methodology of the Progeny forever.
Segment 1: Recap and the Horizons of Ashura

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens the second episode with a heartfelt poetic lamentation directed at Husayn. He reminds the audience that Ashura is a manifestation of the reality of Husayn, which must be understood through what he calls the "Conscious Reading."

When I stand at the thresholds of the edifice of your majesty, O Abu Abdullah. My thoughts stutter and my words are lost, their letters dancing in pain, for the bird dances slaughtered from pain. And my memory fades, and I return to you and from you, O Husayn, our expressions are diverse and your beauty is one. Our expressions are diverse and your beauty is one, and all point to that very beauty. Moisten your lips with the sweet water of prayers upon Muhammad and the family of Muhammad.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Peace be upon the stations of your holiness, O Husayn. Peace be upon the courtyards of your purity. Peace be upon every lowland and highland that leads me to you from between the folds of the hearts, the light of your splendor and your beauty. Peace be upon your spilled blood. Peace be upon your dyed grey hair. Peace be upon the dust-covered cheek. Peace be upon you, O beloved, O most beloved of all beloveds, O Husayn.

Would that what is between me and you is prosperous. Would that what is between me and you is prosperous, and between me and the worlds is in ruins. And would that you are sweet while life is bitter, and would that you are sweet while life is bitter, and would that you are pleased while the creatures are angry.

The speech in the past night was an index of the titles that I will speak about in this night and in the nights that follow it. Briefly, I point to what I mentioned in the past night. It is an answer to a question: **What is Ashura?** And I said that the answer falls into horizons.

The first horizon is that Ashura is a giant divine project, having a short-term objective, a middle, medium-term objective, and a long-term objective. Its short-term objective is uncovering the truth. And its medium-term objective is preserving the permanence of the Pure Progeny. And its long objective is the civilization of civilizations, the Mahdawi project. The second horizon is reading Husayn through the lens of the **conscious reading**—the methodology of: Husayn who is Husayn.
Segment 2: The Short-Term Objective and the Prophet’s Evaluation

Sheikh al-Ghizzi details the short-term objective of Ashura, citing Sunni sources to argue that the vast majority of the companions turned back on their heels and will be driven away on the Day of Resurrection.

The short-term objective is uncovering the truth which the Ummah devolved to after the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family. There are hundreds and hundreds of proofs and witnesses that speak about the coup of the Ummah. The narrations are clear in **Al-Bukhari and in Muslim**, addressing the description of the Basin of the Messenger of Allah on the Day of Resurrection. The number of those who are saved on that day, as the narration of Al-Bukhari said, is like the **strayed livestock**.

Perhaps one out of ten thousand. Strayed livestock means the animals lost in the desert. These are the ones who will be saved on the Day of Resurrection. And the Prophet, as in their narrations, says to the rest: **"Away with you, away with you. Distance, distance."** The Messenger of Allah speaks to us that they have turned back on their heels.

The last evaluation of the Messenger of Allah for the majestic companions in the issue of the **Calamity of Thursday**, is that the Prophet expelled them. He said to them: **"Get up, get out."** And he was angry, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, when they showed bad manners with him, so they said that the Messenger is hallucinating.

According to their books, the Prophet died on Monday and was buried on Wednesday. When he expelled them, certainly this expulsion will reflect upon their souls. Therefore, the narration from Aisha says that we did not know of the burial of the Prophet until we heard the sound of the shovels. This is the condition of the Ummah in the final moments.
Segment 3: Malik bin Nuwayrah and the Wars of Apostasy

Sheikh al-Ghizzi details the story of Malik bin Nuwayrah, whose Refusal to recognize Abu Bakr led to his murder. He argues that these events exemplify the Ummah's true deviation and turning back on their heels from the path of the Ahlul Bayt.

In the time of Abu Bakr, **Malik bin Nuwayrah** was a man from the people of Paradise. The Prophet appointed him as a Zakat worker. He returned to Medina carrying the funds of Zakat and found that the Prophet had passed away. He said I will not pay them except to the caliph of the Messenger of Allah [Ali]. Therefore Abu Bakr issued the fatwa that the withholder of Zakat is a disbeliever. This fatwa is present in the books of the people.

He sent his forces, **Khalid bin Al-Walid** and his deputy Dirar bin Al-Azwar. Khalid arrived at the encampments, they all prayed. Khalid agreed with his companions: **"O assembly of Muslims warm your companions."** Meaning, let every one of you rise to his host and kill him. Khalid killed Malik bin Nuwayrah and cut off his head, making it an **Ufiyah** (a stand for a cooking pot).

And he committed adultery with his wife on that night. The people say that Khalid exercised **ijtihad** and erred in ijtihad. And Abu Bakr, in his lack of punishing Khalid, also exercised ijtihad. We remain in this trajectory from a flawed ijtihad to a flawed ijtihad. This is a manifestation of the manifestations of the true turning back on the heels when they left the door that Allah opened for them and went searching in **dark holes.**
Segment 4: The Farce of Exile and the Persecution of the Loyal

Sheikh al-Ghizzi continues to provide historical examples of the Ummah's deviation. He references the Ziyarah Al-Jamiah to highlight the persecution of the Prophet's loyal companions, focusing on the story of Abu Dharr Al-Ghifari, who was exiled to the desolate land of Al-Rabadhah.

When Al-Fuja'ah Al-Sulami was brought to Abu Bakr, he issued the ruling to burn him with fire. Al-Tabari says he was thrown into the fire bound. They said that Abu Bakr exercised ijtihad. In the days of the caliph Umar, the same operations continued. I point to the story of **Nasr bin Hajjaj**. Because he was handsome, the ruling was issued from the caliph that he exiled him from Medina. He shaved his hair, wrapped a turban on him, and eventually exiled him for his beauty.

In the **Ziyarah Al-Jamiah for the Imams of the Believers**, we read: **"And tied its Salman, and drove away its Miqdad, and exiled its Jundub."** Jundub is the name of **Abu Dharr Al-Ghifari**. He was exiled from Medina to Al-Rabadhah by Uthman. What is the crime of Abu Dharr? His truthful defense and his decisive stance in the allegiance of the Master of Legatees.
Segment 5: The Deaths of Abu Dharr and Ammar bin Yasir

Sheikh al-Ghizzi describes the tragic end of Abu Dharr in the desert. He then shifts to the treatment of Ammar bin Yasir by the caliph Uthman, who personally kicked Ammar in the stomach and chest, causing severe injuries.

Abu Dharr Al-Ghifari passed away in that desert before the hands of his young daughter. Hunger, illness, and old age intensified upon him. The Messenger of Allah had informed him that a group of people coming from Iraq would undertake his burial. Indeed, **Malik al-Ashtar** approached and took charge.

The Ziyarah says: **"...and ripped open the stomach of its Ammar."** Ammar bin Yasir entered upon Uthman to hand him a letter from the companions regarding his corruption. Uthman said: "What made you so bold against me, O son of **Al-Mutakka'ah**?" Marwan advised him to kill him. They stretched him on the ground. Uthman was kicking him on his groin with his foot until a **hernia** occurred and his ribs were broken.
Segment 6: Media Warfare and the Umayyad Deviation

Sheikh al-Ghizzi discusses the Umayyad era, asserting that the Umayyads pioneered the use of sophisticated Roman-style media warfare and propaganda to target the Ahlul Bayt. The tragedy of Ashura, he explains, was the ultimate stand to completely expose this deep-rooted falsehood.

In their books, **Yazid used to commit adultery with his mother**, his daughters, and his sisters. I say it with a full mouth because I know the sources. Muawiyah and Yazid are one methodology. The Ahlul Bayt were killed by way of **media warfare** before they were killed by swords. Thought is what leads to wars.

Muawiyah benefited from the **Roman culture** in his methods of cunning and media. The Ahlul Bayt were fought with the forgery of the word. After Ashura, a prominent line appeared distinguishing the **Husayni group** from others. Shiism began since Gabriel said **"Read"**; there is no Jaafari sect equated with other sects, there is only the Islam of the Book and the Progeny and the Islam of the people.
Segment 7: The Lamp of Guidance and Inadequate Language

Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes his explanation of the short-term objective, emphasizing that the tragedy revealed hidden truths. He explains that he uses the word "giant" to describe the Ashura project only because vocabulary fails him when trying to encompass a divine sacrifice whose capital was the very blood and head of Husayn.

Written upon the leg of the Throne: **Husayn is a lamp of guidance and an ark of salvation.** A lamp shines and uncovers the darkness. I describe the Ashura project as **giant** only because I find no other word. A project whose capital is the head of Husayn. Language stutters and thought stutters in the presence of Husayn.
Segment 8: The Medium-Term Objective and the Strategy of Weeping

Sheikh al-Ghizzi transitions to the medium-term objective: preserving the methodology of the Book and the Progeny. He offers a profound explanation for why the subsequent Imams heavily emphasized weeping for Husayn: weeping is not merely an emotional release, but a strategic and spiritual mechanism designed to tether the soul to the Ahlul Bayt.

The **medium-term objective** is preserving the permanence of the methodology of the Book and the Progeny. Husayn had an eye on everything and an eye on **Imam Al-Sajjad**, the father of the family. The intermediate objective is the permanence of this methodology. Therefore, you notice this urging from the Imams upon **weeping for Husayn**.

**Weeping is a means of connection to the Master of Martyrs.** The state of joy and the state of sadness are the strongest states that control the human entity. The Imams urged upon the issue of weeping and visitation as a **fundamental link**. When the days of Husayn come, the Shiite tendency manifests even in those who have forgotten.
Segment 9: Perfection of Religion and the Final Bead of the Rosary

Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes by asserting the Ashura project successfully preserved the methodology of the Progeny. He narrates the story of Sa'id bin Murrah Al-Tamimi, who answered the Imam's final call, becoming the completing bead in the rosary of the martyrs.

The project of Ashura has achieved its objectives one hundred percent. **"Today I have perfected for you your religion."** This perfection is by this methodology. The third verse and the sixty-seventh verse of Surat Al-Ma'idah are from one Surah, talking about this reality. This night is the night of the companions. **"I do not know companions more loyal nor better than my companions."**

**Sa'id bin Murrah Al-Tamimi** arrived in the final moments. Husayn was standing in the middle of the battlefield looking around, waiting for the final bead of the rosary to be complete. Sa'id stood at the tent and greeted the family. The Wise Lady came out and said: **"Indeed Husayn is waiting for you."**

Sa'id advanced to the battle and the swords cut him into pieces. Husayn returned, standing between the scattered body parts, calling out his brothers and sons. O Lord of Husayn, by the right of Husayn, heal the chest of Husayn with the appearance of Al-Hujjah. O Allah, make us live the life of Husayn and write us in the service of Husayn and the family of Husayn.

title: The File of Ashura - Episode 3: The Long-Term Mahdawi Vision, Yazid’s Depravity, and the Staggering Wealth of the Companions
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Video Overview: Episode 3

In this third episode, Sheikh al-Ghizzi explores the **long-term objective** of the Ashura project: the preparation for the global **Mahdawi civilization**. He pauses to address historical depravity, citing undeniable Sunni sources like **Muruj al-Dhahab** and **Al-Mustadrak** to document **Yazid’s immorality**, specifically reports of incest and wine-drinking. The Sheikh then conducts a fascinating financial audit of the companions, revealing the staggering wealth accumulated by figures like **Zubayr bin al-Awwam**, whose estate he calculates at a modern value of approximately **7.7 billion dollars**. He argues that this shift toward extreme materialism and the **Ominous Document** (al-Sahifah) were the primary drivers of the Ummah's deviation. The episode concludes with a tribute to the bravery and loyalty of **Al-Abbas**, the Moon of the Hashimites.
Segment 1: Recap and the Mahdawi Horizon

Sheikh al-Ghizzi begins the third episode with an evocative poetic supplication, expressing a thirst for the love of Husayn that can never be quenched. He transitions into a systematic review of the Giant Divine Project of Ashura, recapping the short-term and medium-term objectives. He introduces the focus of this episode: the long-term objective, which he identifies as the Mahdawi project—the ultimate civilization of peace, mercy, and guidance led by the Imam of our time.

There it takes me far away, O Abu Abdullah, and his intimate supplication glimmers between the pericardium and the sisters of the soul. So I lose myself between my burning grief and the radiance. I drank your love and was not satisfied, and thirst still stares at me from every direction.

I drank love cup after cup, the drink did not run out nor was my thirst quenched. For Husayn, moisten your lips with the ginger-flavored water of prayers upon Muhammad and the family of Muhammad. O Zahra.

Peace be upon the stations of your holiness, O Husayn. Peace be upon the courtyards of your purity. Peace be upon every lowland and highland that leads me to you from between the folds of the hearts, the light of your splendor and your beauty. Peace be upon your spilled blood. Peace be upon your dyed grey hair. Peace be upon the dust-covered cheek. Peace be upon you, O beloved, O most beloved of all beloveds, O Husayn.
I love you with two loves, O Abu Abdullah. I love you with two loves: the love of passion and a love because you are worthy of that.

My speech is still continuing in the answer to the question with which I began these gatherings: What is Ashura? I spoke about the **short-term objective** (uncovering falsehood) and the **medium-term objective** (maintaining the methodology of the Book and the Progeny). The speech remains about the **long-term objective**, the **Mahdawi project**—the **civilization of civilizations**, the project of happiness, peace, mercy, and guidance led by the Hujjah son of Al-Hasan Al-Askari.
Segment 2: Scientific Integrity and Yazid’s Immorality

Sheikh al-Ghizzi addresses the feedback from the previous lecture where he mentioned Yazid’s acts of incest. He argues that the Shia do not need to fabricate lies because the virtue of the Ahlul Bayt and the depravity of their enemies are both inherently extreme and well-documented. He asserts his commitment to scientific integrity as a follower of Imam Jafar al-Sadiq. He then introduces Muruj al-Dhahab by Al-Mas'udi to establish Yazid’s reputation for wine drinking and general immorality.

In the night of yesterday, I pointed to a model of falsehood: Muawiyah and Yazid. I said that **Yazid used to commit adultery with his mother, his daughters, and his sisters.** It reached me through discussions that there are those saying these are the lies of the Shia. I say here, they are not lies. The Shia are not in need of lying.

Our Imams possess such perfection that if we spoke and spoke, we would not be able to describe it. And the enemies of our Imams possess such **vileness** that we cannot fulfill its description. We are the students of Jafar bin Muhammad, and our Jafar is **Al-Sadiq**. From him we learned **scientific truthfulness**.

This is **Muruj al-Dhahab** by **Al-Mas'udi**. On page eighty-two, he speaks about Yazid: "And for Yazid are strange reports and many flaws of drinking wine, killing the son of the daughter of the Prophet, and cursing the **Legatee** (Ali)." Al-Mas'udi mentions flaws of drinking wine, destroying the House, and general immorality and debauchery.
Segment 3: Sunni Sources on Yazid’s Incest and Decadence

Sheikh al-Ghizzi continues his presentation of evidence against Yazid, citing several major Sunni works. He references Al-Imama wal-Siyasa to show Yazid’s reputation for public indecency and wine drinking. He then moves to Al-Mustadrak and Tabaqat al-Kubra, citing the testimony of Ma'qil bin Sinan, a companion of the Prophet, and Abdullah bin Hanzalah. He highlights their explicit declarations that Yazid committed incest with his mothers, daughters, and sisters, arguing that these historical records prove the Shia do not need to fabricate claims regarding the depravity of their enemies.

This is **Al-Imama wal-Siyasa** by **Ibn Qutayba al-Dinawari**. On page 189: "Yazid drinks wine and amuses himself with singing girls and he is **reckless with indecencies**." Indecencies (*fawahish*) in Quranic terminology primarily apply to adultery.

Now look at **Al-Mustadrak** by **Al-Hakim al-Nishapuri**. **Ma'qil bin Sinan al-Ashja'i**, a companion of the Prophet, said: "I came out reluctantly for the allegiance of this man. He is a man who drinks wine and **commits adultery with the haram**." What is the haram? Surat al-Nisa explicitly lists mothers, daughters, and sisters.
The same speech came in **Al-Tabaqat al-Kubra** by **Ibn Sa'd**. Page thirty-three, the statement of the Medina revolution led by **Abdullah bin Hanzalah**:
By Allah, we did not revolt against Yazid until we feared we would be pelted with stones from the sky. He is a man who marries mothers, daughters, and sisters, drinks wine, and abandons the prayer.
These are the books of the people and this is their speech. There is not in these books even one single line from Shiite books.
Segment 4: The Triad of Conflict and the Poverty of Zubayr

Sheikh al-Ghizzi explores the deep-rooted causes of the Ummah’s corruption, identifying a "triad of conflict" that drives human history: sex, wealth, and power. He asserts that the Ummah's deviation was a result of these primal drives, following the patterns of previous nations. To illustrate the extreme financial shift, he introduces the case of Zubayr bin al-Awwam, a companion who transitioned from near-poverty during the Prophet's time to amassing astronomical wealth.

The problem of humanity since its first day is the triad of **sex, wealth, and power**. This is the axis of the clash of civilizations. The Seal of the Prophets said: "Step by step, and inch by inch, and even if they entered a lizard’s hole, you would enter it."

I point to an example: **Zubayr bin al-Awwam**. This is **Sahih al-Bukhari**, hadith 5224. Asma bint Abi Bakr, his wife, said: "Zubayr married me, and he had no wealth on earth, nor a slave, nor anything other than a water-drawing camel and his horse." She says she used to knead dough and bake for him because he was so poor.
Segment 5: The Astronomical Estates of Zubayr

Sheikh al-Ghizzi contrasts Zubayr’s early poverty with the staggering wealth he accumulated by the time of his death, as recorded in Muruj al-Dhahab. He details that Zubayr owned massive commercial estates in Basra, Kufa, Egypt, and Alexandria—buildings that remained functional and famous for over three centuries. He highlights that Zubayr left behind one thousand thoroughbred horses, one thousand slaves, and vast tracts of land across various regions, illustrating the profound shift toward worldliness.

Let us see how the situation of Zubayr became. **Al-Mas'udi** in **Muruj al-Dhahab** says: "He built his house in Basra—a **massive commercial institution**... Merchants and masters of wealth from Bahrain inhabit it." He died in 40 AH, but these buildings were still famous and used in 332 AH.

Zubayr left behind fifty thousand dinars in cash and **one thousand noble horses**. Even in our time, noble Arabian horses sell for millions of dollars. He also left **one thousand slaves** who brought him the land tax (**Kharaj**) daily. This is mentioned in *Al-Isti'ab* and *Usd al-Ghabah*.
Segment 6: The 7.7 Billion Dollar Fortune

Sheikh al-Ghizzi performs a detailed financial audit of Zubayr’s estate, translating the historical figures into modern economic terms. By calculating the value of his thousands of slaves, horses, and vast real estate holdings, he estimates the fortune to be worth approximately 7.7 billion US dollars. He argues that this staggering wealth explains why Zubayr "forgot" the Prophet’s warnings and chose to fight against Imam Ali, as material interests naturally blind a person to spiritual truths.

I tried to calculate these funds at the lowest possible estimate in modern currency. If we assume he owned only ten houses, each worth 600,000 dinars (a price he was once cheated with), and add the daily income of 1,000 slaves and the value of 1,000 noble horses—the result is **ten million mithqals of gold**.
By calculating the purchasing power and modern gold rates, the result is a fortune of **seven billion and seven hundred and forty-one million dollars ($7.7 Billion).**

These **billions make one forget everything.** In the event of the Camel, Ali asked him: "Do you remember what the Messenger of Allah said?" Zubayr wept and said he had forgotten. Material billions cause that type of amnesia.
Segment 7: The Luxury of Abdullah bin Umar

Sheikh al-Ghizzi provides further context regarding the accumulation of wealth among second-tier supporters like Zayd bin Thabit, whose estate was so vast it was broken with axes. He contrasts this with the transformation of Abdullah bin Umar, who once lived as a pauper in the Prophet’s Mosque but eventually became wealthy enough to adorn his female slaves with gold—a level of luxury usually reserved for pampered royalty.

**Zayd bin Thabit** died leaving behind so much gold and silver that it was **broken with axes**. As for **Abdullah bin Umar**, *Sahih al-Bukhari* says he used to sleep as a pauper in the Mosque of the Prophet. But look at **Al-Muwatta** of Malik bin Anas: "Abdullah bin Umar used to **adorn his female slaves with gold**."

Adorning slaves with gold is a level of **pampered luxury** usually found only in decadent royal courts. Umar once saw a female slave dressed as a free woman and beat her, but he did not beat his son's slaves; he only complained to Hafsa about it. This was the shift in morality after the Prophet.
Segment 8: The Ominous Document and the Theft of Inheritance

Sheikh al-Ghizzi connects the accumulation of wealth to the Ominous Document (al-Sahifah al-Mash'umah), which he argues was the strategic blueprint to divert leadership away from the Ahlul Bayt. He critiques the historical narrative surrounding the inheritance of Fatima al-Zahra, pointing out contradictions in Sahih al-Bukhari where the Prophet’s wives—contrary to the claim that "Prophets do not leave inheritance"—initially sought their share of the estate.

The **Ominous Document** (al-Sahifah) was the blueprint for Karbala. Imam al-Sadiq said: "If the document is written, Husayn is killed." This document fueled the **theft of inheritance**. Look at *Sahih al-Bukhari*, page 1190: Fatima demanded her right and was rejected.

But look at the contradiction in *Sahih al-Bukhari*: **The wives of the Prophet initially wanted to send Uthman to Abu Bakr to ask for their inheritance.** They demanded their **eighth**—a specific legal portion. This proves the people of the house and the wives believed in inheritance. Aisha was the one who rejected them with the fabricated hadith: "We do not leave inheritance; what we leave is charity."
Segment 9: Aisha’s Inheritance and the Forgetful Prophet

Sheikh al-Ghizzi highlights a contradiction: while Fatima was denied her inheritance, Aisha was allowed to possess burial rights in the Prophet's house. He presents a narration from Sahih al-Bukhari where the Prophet is depicted as "forgetting" verses of the Quran, arguing that such a portrayal of a negligent Prophet is a result of the Ummah's deviation. He concludes that Husayn's blood was the ultimate force that exposed this systematic forgery.

While Fatima was denied, **Aisha was allowed to inherit burial rights** in the house. When Umar was dying, he sent to Aisha to ask permission to be buried there. How did she own the land if there is no inheritance? This is the **forgery of truths**.

They even depict the Prophet in *Sahih al-Bukhari* as someone who **forgets verses of the Quran**. Aisha narrates that the Prophet heard a man reciting and said: "He has certainly reminded me of such and such verse, I had dropped them." This portrayal of a **negligent Prophet** is the fruit of the coup. The blood of Husayn was spilled to draw a **red line** and uncover this systematic forgery.
Segment 10: The Long-Term Objective and the Bravery of Al-Abbas

Sheikh al-Ghizzi introduces the long-term objective of the Ashura project: preparing the foundations for the global Mahdawi project. He provides a linguistic analysis of the name "Abbas," explaining it as the "lion from whom other lions flee." He concludes the lecture with a powerful narration of Al-Abbas’s martyrdom, emphasizing his unwavering loyalty.

The **long-term objective** of Ashura is the **Mahdawi project**. Tonight is the night of **Abu al-Fadl al-Abbas**. According to *Lisan al-Arab*, the name **Abbas** means: **the lion from whom other lions flee.**

Al-Abbas was the **cause of joy and security** in the tents. When Husayn said to him, "Ride, may I be sacrificed for you," he meant "may the soul of Muhammad be sacrificed for you." The children of Husayn were **gasping from thirst**. Al-Abbas reached the watering place and pushed away four thousand soldiers single-handedly.

His most bewildered moment was when both his hands were cut and the **arrows fell on the water skin**, spilling the water. One speaker in Najaf saw the Master of Believers in a vision, saying: "You did not mention my greatest calamity—a horseman falls with his hands, but how did I meet the earth while both my hands were cut?"
O Lord of Husayn, by the right of Husayn, heal the chest of Husayn with the appearance of **Al-Hujjah**. O Allah, do not separate between us and Husayn.

title: The File of Ashura - Episode 4: The Husayni Incubator and the Failure of Immediate Reform
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Video Overview: Episode 4

In this fourth episode, Sheikh al-Ghizzi conducts a deep critique of the concept of **Reform** in the context of Ashura. He argues that no immediate social or political reform was realized following Karbala; instead, the Ummah plummeted into even deeper moral and political degradation, typified by the **Umayyad Caliph al-Walid bin Yazid** and his desecration of the Quran. Utilizing the **Ziyarah of al-Nahiyah al-Muqaddasah**, the Sheikh establishes that the true reform Husayn sought was not a worldly political victory, but the creation of a **Husayni Incubator**—a protected spiritual environment for the "Husayni group" to preserve the methodology of the Progeny. This incubator serves as the necessary foundation for the **long-term objective**: the global Mahdawi project. The episode concludes with the heart-wrenching martyrdom of the **children of Imam al-Hasan**.
Segment 1: The Formal Testament and the Long-Term Objective

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens the fourth episode with a poetic lamentation and a direct spiritual greeting to Imam Husayn. He recaps the short-term and medium-term objectives of Ashura, emphasizing that the "Clarity" of the Ahlul Bayt's methodology is its most defining trait. He then focuses on the long-term objective: the Mahdawi project, identifying three key pillars in Imam Husayn’s testament: seeking Reform, Enjoining the Good, and following the Sunnah of the Prophet and Ali.

The breeze of your wounds, O Abu Abdullah, has begun to surround us, and here we are approaching little by little the Day of Ashura. In the hadiths of the People of the House of Infallibility: Whoever wishes to visit Husayn from afar, let him say three times: **"May Allah bless you, O Abu Abdullah."**

The speech has reached us in answering the question: "What is Ashura?" to the third objective, the **long-term objective**. Ashura, that giant divine project—its **short-term objective**: uncovering the falsehood; its **medium-term objective**: preserving the permanence of the methodology. Its **long-term objective**: establishing the rules, principles, and firm foundations for the divine **Mahdawi project**.
The most important trait in the methodology of Ali and the family of Ali is the trait of **Clarity**. The Master of Martyrs, in his formal testament to Muhammad bin al-Hanafiyyah, stated:
I have only gone out to seek reform in the Ummah of my grandfather. I want to enjoin the good and forbid the evil, and to proceed by the Sunnah of my grandfather and my father Ali bin Abi Talib.
Segment 2: The Failure of Immediate Social Reform

Sheikh al-Ghizzi critiques the common interpretation of "reform," arguing that no immediate reform was realized in the Ummah’s collective consciousness or political state following Ashura. He points to the decrease in supporters for later Imams and the moral decay in Mecca and Medina under Umayyad rule as evidence that the deviation only intensified.

The question: after Ashura, was anything of reform realized in this Ummah? The answer: **never.** Not a single thing of reform was realized. If there were any reform, it would appear in the **consciousness of the nation.**

Their narrations gather upon one truth: that the one among us with the **most supporters is the Master of Martyrs.** The Imams after him did not have supporters equal to his number, let alone more. **Imam al-Baqir** said: "By Allah, if I found four pious ones, I would speak." **Imam al-Sadiq** told Sudayr al-Sayrafi that if he had supporters equal to seventeen goats, he would rise.

Socially, the period in which corruption spread most was in **Mecca and Medina after Ashura.** A review of *al-Aghani* shows that most female singers and dancers were in the holy cities. Even the Master of Legatees warned that **after the seventy (AH), corruptions would spread.** On a political level, there was no immediate reform; corruption only increased.
Segment 3: Political Degradation in the Umayyad Caliphate

Sheikh al-Ghizzi provides a stark illustration of the political and moral degradation of the Umayyad caliphate by citing an account from Muruj al-Dhahab. He narrates a scandalous incident involving Caliph al-Walid bin Yazid to demonstrate that Husayn’s reform was not a political success in the immediate, worldly sense.

I read to you an example from **Muruj al-Dhahab** by Al-Mas'udi regarding the Caliph of the Muslims, **al-Walid bin Yazid.** A man narrated being with al-Walid and a singer from Mecca. The Caliph, in an extreme fit of desire, threw himself upon the singer, kissing him in a highly indecent manner.

I say these words so it may be known who those who antagonized the Ahlul Bayt were. These are the "Twelve Imams from Quraysh" that the opponents try to equate with the Infallibles. The Caliph even took off his clothes and threw them upon the singer, remaining **naked** until new clothes were brought.
Segment 4: Desecration of the Quran and the Ominous Document

Sheikh al-Ghizzi recounts the absolute desecration of the Quran by al-Walid bin Yazid, who used the holy book as a target for archery. He argues that this trajectory of corruption was the logical execution of the "Ominous Document" signed by the companions, proving Husayn’s reform served a deeper purpose.

Al-Walid bin Yazid once opened the Mushaf and read verses about stubborn tyrants. He called for the Mushaf and set it up as a **target for arrows**, saying: **"When you meet your Lord on the Day of Resurrection, say: 'O Lord, al-Walid tore me apart.'"**

This al-Walid is a **legitimate political birth** extending back to the **Saqifah**. **Imam al-Sadiq** said: "If the document is written, Husayn is killed." Husayn was killed from the day of that document. If the Master of Martyrs says he went out to seek "Reform," he clearly aims for another meaning than immediate political change.
Segment 5: The Collapse of Values in the Ziyarah of al-Nahiyah

Sheikh al-Ghizzi cites the Ziyarah of al-Nahiyah al-Muqaddasah to provide a definitive answer regarding "reform" after Ashura. The Ziyarah describes a total collapse of Islamic values following Husayn's martyrdom, arguing that the reform Husayn sought must point to a meaning far deeper than immediate political change.
There is a clear text from the **Hujjah bin al-Hasan** in the **Ziyarah of al-Nahiyah al-Muqaddasah**:

They have certainly killed Islam by killing you, and they suspended the prayer and the fasting, and they violated the Sunnahs and the rulings... And the Book of Allah has returned abandoned. There appeared after you the change, the alteration, the atheism, the suspension, the whims, the delusions, the seditions, and the falsehoods.
If all these things were lost, what reform was realized for the general Ummah? The change and alteration appeared **after** Husayn. Therefore, the reform he sought was directed elsewhere.
Segment 6: Cosmic Reform and the Wilayah as "The Good"

Sheikh al-Ghizzi analyzes the paradox of "Enjoining the Good" in Karbala, where standard legal conditions were not met. He argues that Husayn's actions point to a cosmic reality where "Good" is synonymous with the Wilayah of the Infallible Imam.

Enjoining the good is a jurisprudential issue requiring the condition of **Effectiveness**. If we look at the losses in Karbala, they cannot be balanced against any immediate worldly result. If we sharpen the sight, the true **"Good"** in the narrations of the Progeny is the **Infallible Imam.**

**Enjoining the Good is enjoining the Wilayah of the Infallible Imam.** Every good mentioned in the Quran is the Ahlul Bayt. What is the value of prayer without the Wilayah of Ali? **There is no difference between prayer and adultery if stripped of the Wilayah.** This is the path that leads us to the long-term Mahdawi project.
Segment 7: The Main Station and the Husayni Methodology

Sheikh al-Ghizzi explains that the "Reform" was directed specifically at the "Husayni group." He argues that the Imams created a unique spiritual environment, asserting that all paths in the school of the Progeny lead to Husayn, as his project provides the cradle for the Mahdawi civilization.

The reform the Imam sought was for the **Husayni group**—the ones distinguished by the short-term objective. In our culture, we say: **All roads lead to Husayn.** The first subject for the People of the House is named Husayn because his project leads directly to the Imam of our Time.

The permanence of this methodology is by Husayn. **Bihar al-Anwar** is 110 volumes, and all of it pushes toward Husayn. This project achieved its objectives one hundred percent; the fact that we are gathered here in the west of the earth while Husayn is in the east is proof that the methodology continues.
Segment 8: The Non-Negotiable Pillar of Visitation

Sheikh al-Ghizzi highlights a unique exception: while the Imams generally prioritized physical safety through taqiyyah, they encouraged the visitation of Imam Husayn even in the face of mortal danger. He compares the Husayni mission to a protective "incubator" for the Mahdawi project.

The Imams always preserve the lives of their followers, yet when it comes to Husayn, they push them toward **visitation even in the face of death.** The Master of Martyrs created a special **Incubator.**

Just as a seed needs a specific environment to bear fruit, Husayn created this incubator to preserve the continuation of life. Through this incubator, the new newborn will emerge: the **Mahdawi program.**
Segment 9: The Spiritual GPS of the Ziyarat

Sheikh al-Ghizzi emphasizes the necessity of studying the texts of the Visitations as the "literary core" of Shia culture, acting as the spiritual GPS. He argues that the true "Food" of the soul is the word of the Infallible, while lectures are merely the tableware used to serve it.

Shiite culture lacks the study of the **Visitations (Ziyarat).** These texts are concentrated, strong brochures of truth. The hadith of the People of the House is the **GPS device** that leads you to the station. Lectures are merely the vessels; the good food is the speech of the Infallible.
Segment 10: The Martyrdom of the Children of al-Hasan

Sheikh al-Ghizzi focuses on the immense significance of Imam al-Hasan’s children in Karbala. He details the heart-wrenching martyrdom of al-Qasim and Abdullah bin al-Hasan, concluding with an emotional lamentation for the fallen members of the Household.

Tonight is the night of **al-Qasim.** Just as Husayn offered Ali al-Akbar, Imam al-Hasan was present in Karbala through his children. **Abdullah bin al-Hasan** was also slaughtered by Harmala between the hands of his uncle.

Al-Qasim was a youth whose face was like a piece of the moon. He leaned down in the middle of battle to tie his sandal—the son of the Prophet disdained to go barefoot. When he was struck, he cried: **"O Uncle, reach me!"** Husayn manifested like a hawk. The Imam said: "O Qasim, it is hard upon your uncle that you call him and he does not answer."

A child escaped the tents—Abdullah bin al-Hasan—running toward his uncle. As a sword was raised, he received it with his hand, severing it. Husayn took him to his chest, but Harmala slaughtered him there. The pains of Husayn cannot be described. Peace be upon the withered lips. Peace be upon you, O Husayn.
